CRISPR technology is revolutionizing the improvement of tomato and other fruit crops
====================================================================================

*   [Tian Wang](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#auth-Tian-Wang-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#Aff1), 
*   [Hongyan Zhang](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#auth-Hongyan-Zhang-Aff2)[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#Aff2)&
*   [Hongliang Zhu](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#auth-Hongliang-Zhu-Aff3)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-1484-2212](http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1484-2212)[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#Aff3)

[_Horticulture Research_](https://www.nature.com/hortres)**volume 6**, Article number:77 (2019) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#citeas)

*   70k Accesses

*   146 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Fruits are major sources of essential nutrients and serve as staple foods in some areas of the world. The increasing human population and changes in climate experienced worldwide make it urgent to the production of fruit crops with high yield and enhanced adaptation to the environment, for which conventional breeding is unlikely to meet the demand. Fortunately, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR) technology paves the way toward a new horizon for fruit crop improvement and consequently revolutionizes plant breeding. In this review, the mechanism and optimization of the CRISPR system and its application to fruit crops, including resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses, fruit quality improvement, and domestication are highlighted. Controversies and future perspectives are discussed as well.

Introduction
------------

Fruits are major sources of fibers, vitamins, and minerals worldwide[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR1 "Giovannoni, J., Nguyen, C., Ampofo, B., Zhong, S. & Fei, Z. The epigenome and transcriptional dynamics of fruit ripening. Annu. Rev. Plant Biol. 68, 61–84 (2017)."). In some parts of Asia, Africa, and South America, banana, breadfruit, and date fruit also serve as staple foods[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR2 "Ramdath, D. D., Isaacs, R. L., Teelucksingh, S. & Wolever, T. M. Glycaemic index of selected staples commonly eaten in the Caribbean and the effects of boiling v. crushing. Br. J. Nutr. 91, 971–977 (2004)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR3 "Vayalil, P. K. Date fruits (Phoenix dactylifera Linn): an emerging medicinal food. Crit. Rev. Food Sci. 52, 249–271 (2012)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR4 "Singh, B., Singh, J. P., Kaur, A. & Singh, N. Bioactive compounds in banana and their associated health benefits— a review. Food Chem. 206, 1–11 (2016)."). Fruit crops are at high risk under climate change[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR5 "Karkute, S. G., Singh, A. K., Gupta, O. P., Singh, P. M. & Singh, B. CRISPR/Cas9 mediated genome engineering for improvement of horticultural Crops. Front. Plant Sci. 8, 01635 (2017)."). To increase the chances of a steady fruit supply, our ancestors domesticated wild plant species into cultivated crops. Following the “rediscovery” of Mendel’s laws in 1900, breeders started selecting and crossing superior plants[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR6 "Hickey, J. M. et al. Genomic prediction unifies animal and plant breeding programs to form platforms for biological discovery. Nat. Genet. 49, 1297–1303 (2017)."). However, conventional breeding has major shortcomings. First, it largely depends on existing natural allelic variations and is thus inefficient for obtaining the desired characteristics by random mixing of tens of thousands of genes[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR5 "Karkute, S. G., Singh, A. K., Gupta, O. P., Singh, P. M. & Singh, B. CRISPR/Cas9 mediated genome engineering for improvement of horticultural Crops. Front. Plant Sci. 8, 01635 (2017)."). Although conventional breeding has increased crop productivity, it is often accompanied by loss of fitness and genetic diversity[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR7 "Meyer, R. S. & Purugganan, M. D. Evolution of crop species: genetics of domestication and diversification. Nat. Rev. Genet. 14, 840–852 (2013)."), and it is a rather time-consuming practice that could hardly ensure a sufficient food supply for the rapidly growing human population around the world[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR8 "Tester, M. & Langridge, P. Breeding technologies to increase crop production in a Changing world. Science 327, 818–822 (2010)."). Therefore, continuous technological innovation is required to meet the increasing demands of consumers[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR9 "Bigliardi, B. & Galati, F. Innovation trends in the food industry: the case of functional foods. Trends Food Sci. Technol. 31, 118–129 (2013).").

Genetic engineering techniques have numerous applications in fruit crops, as they allow improvement of important agronomic traits such as biotic and abiotic stress tolerance and fruit quality. During the past two decades, several fruit crops have been modified using these techniques. In contrast to conventional breeding, recombinant DNA technology allows transfer of the desired genes from any organism, plant or microorganism into fruit crops, extending the opportunities for fruit yield enhancement by offering new genotypes and phenotypes for breeding purposes, and ultimately improving fruit quality as well as enhancing shelf life. Thus, genetic engineering has been ranked as the fastest developing technology in agriculture[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR10 "Parmar, N. et al. Genetic engineering strategies for biotic and abiotic stress tolerance and quality enhancement in horticultural crops: a comprehensive review. 3 Biotech 7, 239 (2017)."). The organisms obtained by recombinant DNA technology are termed “genetically modified” (GM). In 1994, the transgenic “Flavr Savr tomato” was approved for commercial growth in the United States (US) by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). The modification it contained allowed a slowing of its ripening process and prevented it from softening after picking. The GM papaya authorized for marketing can resist ring spot virus attacks and show enhanced productivity. Eighty percent of Hawaiian papaya produced today is genetically engineered, and no alternative method is available[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR11 "Bawa, A. S. & Anilakumar, K. R. Genetically modified foods: safety, risks and public concerns—a review. J. Food Sci. Technol. 50, 1035–1046 (2013).").

However, the development of new GM crops is largely affected by regulatory-approval processes because the purpose of the approval system is preventing harm to human health and the environment, as well as avoiding economic losses[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR12 "Gould, F. et al. in Genetically Engineered Crops: Experiences and Prospects (eds Board on Agriculture and Natural Resources) Ch. 6 (National Academies Press, Washington, DC, 2016)."). These regulations also help ensure consumer confidence in GM crop biosafety[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR13 "Millstone, E., Stirling, A. & Glover, D. Regulating genetic engineering the limits and politics of knowledge. Issues Sci. Technol. 31, 23–26 (2015)."). As a result, the costs of obtaining approval for new GM crops can be very high, and the regulatory requirements may also delay product marketing[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR14 "Falck-Zepeda, J. et al. Estimates and implications of the costs of compliance with biosafety regulations in developing countries. GM Crops Food 3, 52–59 (2012)."). Jefferson et al.[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR15 "Jefferson, D. J., Graff, G. D., Chi-Ham, C. L. & Bennett, A. B. The emergence of agbiogenerics. Nat. Biotechnol. 33, 819–823 (2015).") have argued that these stringent regulations can result in unnecessary barriers to the introduction of new GM crops. Thus, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR) technology may be a better choice: in 2016, a CRISPR-edited mushroom escaped US regulation as it fell outside the GM organisms legislation by not containing foreign DNA[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR16 "Kim, J. & Kim, J. Bypassing GMO regulations with CRISPR gene editing. Nat. Biotechnol. 34, 1014–1015 (2016)."). In 2017, the FDA approved the marketing of a false flax with increased oil content and a drought-tolerant soybean[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR17 "Waltz, E. With a free pass, CRISPR-edited plants reach market in record time. Nat. Biotechnol. 36, 6–7 (2018)."), indicating that the CRISPR-edited crops were not under the same stringent regulations as traditional GM crops and that the CRISPR technology would definitely revolutionize the pace of crop breeding[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR18 "Zaidi, S. S., Mukhtar, M. S. & Mansoor, S. Genome editing: targeting susceptibility genes for plant disease resistance. Trends Biotechnol. 36, 898–906 (2018).").

Genome editing has been revolutionized by the development of CRISPR technology
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### The discovery of CRISPR in the prokaryote immune system

The CRISPR system is a sophisticated adaptive immune mechanism present in bacteria and Archaea for defense against invading bacteriophages and exogenous plasmids[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR19 "Sternberg, S. H., Richter, H., Charpentier, E. & Qimron, U. Adaptation in CRISPR-Cas systems. Mol. Cell 61, 797–808 (2016)."). It was first discovered in the genome of _Escherichia coli_ in 1987[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR20 "Ishino, Y., Shinagawa, H., Makino, K., Amemura, M. & Nakata, A. Nucleotide sequence of the iap gene, responsible for alkaline phosphatase isozyme conversion in Escherichia coli, and identification of the gene product. J. Bacteriol. 169, 5429–5433 (1987).") and officially named by the Dutch scientist who identified _CRISPR-associated_ (_Cas_) genes[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR21 "Jansen, R., Embden, J. D. A. V., Gaastra, W. & Schouls, L. M. Identification of genes that are associated with DNA repeats in prokaryotes. Mol. Microbiol. 43, 1565–1575 (2002)."). In 2005, three different research groups simultaneously found that the short sequences of many CRISPR spacers were highly homologous with sequences originating from extra chromosomal DNA[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR22 "Bolotin, A., Ouinquis, B., Sorokin, A. & Ehrlich, S. D. Clustered regularly interspaced short palindrome repeats (CRISPRs) have spacers of extrachromosomal origin. Microbiology 151, 2551–2561 (2005)."),[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR23 "Mojica, F. J. M., Díez-Villaseñor, C. S., García-Martínez, J. S. & Soria, E. Intervening sequences of regularly spaced prokaryotic repeats derive from foreign genetic elements. J. Mol. Evol. 60, 174–182 (2005)."),[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR24 "Pourcel, C., Salvignol, G. & Vergnaud, G. CRISPR elements in Yersinia pestis acquire new repeats by preferential uptake of bacteriophage DNA, and provide additional tools for evolutionary studies. Microbiology 151, 653–663 (2005)."), indicating a relationship between CRISPR and specific immunity. Nearly a decade later, CRISPR-Cas was successfully engineered into an efficient tool to edit human, animal, and plant genomes[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR25 "Shan, Q. et al. Targeted genome modification of crop plants using a CRISPR-Cas system. Nat. Biotechnol. 31, 686–688 (2013)."),[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR26 "Cong, L. et al. Multiplex genome engineering using CRISPR/Cas systems. Science 339, 819–823 (2013)."), extensively boosting its application in fields as diverse as pharmacology, animal domestication, and food science[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR27 "Kaboli, S. & Babazada, H. CRISPR mediated genome engineering and its application in industry. Curr. Issues Mol. Biol. 26, 81–92 (2018).").

A complete CRISPR-Cas locus comprises a CRISPR array that harbors short repetitive elements intercalated with invader DNA-targeting spacers, an AT-rich leader sequence, and an operon of _Cas_ genes encoding the Cas proteins[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR28 "Hille, F. & Charpentier, E. CRISPR-Cas: biology, mechanisms and relevance. Philos. Trans. R. Soc. Ser. B 371, 20150496 (2016)."). Based on the different participating Cas proteins, CRISPR-Cas systems can be categorized into three main types: type I and type III systems use a large multi-Cas protein complex for binding and targeting[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR29 "Nam, K. H. et al. Cas5d protein processes pre-crRNA and assembles into a cascade-like interference complex in subtype I-C/Dvulg CRISPR-Cas system. Structure 20, 1574–1584 (2012)."),[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR30 "Rouillon, C. et al. Structure of the CRISPR interference complex CSM reveals key similarities with cascade. Mol. Cell 52, 124–134 (2013)."), while the type II system requires only a single protein, the CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9), for RNA-guided double-stranded DNA recognition and cleavage using its two distinct domains, RuvC and HNH[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR31 "Gasiunas, G., Barrangou, R., Horvath, P. & Siksnys, V. Cas9-crRNA ribonucleoprotein complex mediates specific DNA cleavage for adaptive immunity in bacteria. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 109, E2579–E2586 (2012)."). The simplicity of the type II CRISPR (i.e., of the CRISPR-Cas9 system) enabled remarkable progress in genome engineering[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR32 "Doudna, J. A. & Charpentier, E. The new frontier of genome engineering with CRISPR-Cas9. Science 346, 1258096 (2014).").

### The mechanism of CRISPR-Cas9

In general, the action of the CRISPR-Cas9 system can be divided into three stages in response to invading foreign DNA[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR33 "Amitai, G. & Sorek, R. CRISPR–Cas adaptation: insights into the mechanism of action. Nat. Rev. Microbiol. 14, 67–76 (2016)."),[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR34 "Wang, H., La Russa, M. & Qi, L. S. CRISPR/Cas9 in genome editing and beyond. Annu. Rev. Biochem. 85, 227–264 (2016)."): (i) acquisition stage—the invading DNA is identified and a spacer sequence derived from the target DNA is inserted into the host CRISPR array to establish immunological memory; (ii) expression stage—the Cas9 protein is expressed, and the CRISPR array is transcribed into a precursor RNA transcript (pre-crRNA). A non-coding trans-activating CRISPR RNA (crRNA) then hybridizes to the pre-crRNA and Cas9 protein and processes them into mature RNA units known as crRNAs; and (iii) interference stage—the mature crRNA guides the Cas9 protein to recognize the appropriate DNA target, leading to the cleavage and degradation of the invading foreign DNA.

The Cas9 protein cuts the DNA to generate a double-strand break (DSB), triggering cellular DNA repair mechanisms (Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#Fig1)). In the absence of a homologous repair template, the error-prone non-homologous end-joining (NHEJ) pathway is activated and introduces random insertions/deletions or even substitutions at the DSB site, generally resulting in the disruption of gene function. Alternatively, if donor DNA template homologous to the sequence surrounding the DSB site is available, the error-free homology-directed repair (HDR) pathway is initiated, leading to mutations that perform precise gene modification, including gene knock-in, deletion, or mutation[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR35 "Yin, K., Gao, C. & Qiu, J. Progress and prospects in plant genome editing. Nat. Plants 3, 17107 (2017)."). At present, the most commonly used Cas9 protein comes from _Streptococcus pyogenes_ (_Sp_)[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR36 "Marraffini, L. A. in Streptococcus pyogenes: Basic Biology to Clinical Manifestations [Internet] (eds Ferretti, J. J., Stevens, D. L. & Fischetti, V. A.) Ch. 11 (University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center, Oklahoma City, 2016)."). To exploit this system for genome editing, synthetic single-guide RNAs (sgRNAs) are required to construct the CRISPR-Cas9 expression cassettes. The Cas9 protein is then guided to specific genomic sites by the sgRNAs that recognize the NGG-type protospacer adjacent motif and targets DNA sequences through Watson–Crick base pairing[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR37 "Liu, X., Xie, C., Si, H. & Yang, J. CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genome editing in plants. Methods 121–122, 94–102 (2017).") (Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1: The mechanism of CRISPR-Cas9-mediated genome engineering in plants.**

[![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41438-019-0159-x/MediaObjects/41438_2019_159_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x/figures/1)

The sgRNA directs the SpCas9 protein to bind genomic DNA through a 20-nucleotide sequence and further guides it to introduce a DSB. This DSB causes random mutations when repaired by the error-prone NHEJ pathway or precise gene modification when repaired by the error-free HDR pathway. CRISPR, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat; Cas, CRISPR-associated; DSB, double-strand break; HDR, homology-directed repair; NHEJ, non-homologous end-joining; sgRNA, single-guide RNA

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x/figures/1)

### The optimization of the CRISPR-Cas system in plants

Since the CRISPR-Cas system was successfully engineered to edit plant genomes in 2013, numerous efforts have been made to transform it into a more powerful tool. At present, CRISPR-Cas has multiplex editing capability, that is, it edits more than one gene at a time[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR38 "Donohoue, P. D., Barrangou, R. & May, A. P. Advances in industrial biotechnology using CRISPR-Cas systems. Trends Biotechnol. 36, 134–146 (2018)."). In addition, CRISPR-Cas can target not only the open reading frame (ORF)[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR39 "Liang, W., Wersch, S., Tong, M. & Li, X. TIR-NB-LRR immune receptor SOC3 pairs with truncated TIR-NB protein CHS1 or TN2 to monitor the homeostasis of E3 ligase SAUL1. New Phytol. 221, 2054–2066 (2018).") and untranslated region[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR40 "Mao, Y. et al. Manipulating plant RNA-silencing pathways to improve the gene editing efficiency of CRISPR/Cas9 systems. Genome Biol. 19, 149 (2018).") of one coding gene but also non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs) including long ncRNA[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR41 "Li, R., Fu, D., Zhu, B., Luo, Y. & Zhu, H. CRISPR/Cas9-mediated mutagenesis of lncRNA1459 alters tomato fruit ripening. Plant J. 94, 513–524 (2018).") and microRNA[42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR42 "Chang, H. et al. CRISPR/cas9, a novel genomic tool to knock down microRNA in vitro and in vivo. Sci. Rep. 6, 22312 (2016)."), as well as promoter regions[43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR43 "Seth, K. & Harish Current status of potential applications of repurposed Cas9 for structural and functional genomics of plants. Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 480, 499–507 (2016)."). Single-base substitutions at genomic targets without requiring DSBs have also been achieved[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR44 "Hess, G. T., Tycko, J., Yao, D. & Bassik, M. C. Methods and applications of CRISPR-mediated base editing in eukaryotic genomes. Mol. Cell 68, 26–43 (2017)."). Here, we describe the optimization of the CRISPR-Cas system regarding the diversified development of Cas proteins, the optimization of Cas promoters, and the empowerment of sgRNAs with multiplexing capability (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#Tab1)).

Applications of CRISPR-Cas9 in fruit crops
------------------------------------------

Duane Green has defined a fruit crop as a perennial, edible crop where the economic product is the true botanical fruit or derived from it[81](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR81 "Rieger, M. in Introduction to Fruit Crops (ed. Rieger, M.) Ch. 1 (Food Products Press, Boca Raton, 2006)."). Some plants, grown primarily as annuals, such as tomatoes, cucumbers, and melons, are also considered fruit crops[82](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR82 "Miller, A. J. & Gross, B. L. From forest to field: perennial fruit crop domestication. Am. J. Bot. 98, 1389–1414 (2011)."). Due to its easily achieved germplasm resources, simple diploid inheritance, efficient breeding, short growing period, ease of genetic transformation, and extensive research, tomato acts as a model for fruit biology[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-019-0159-x#ref-CR1 "Giovannoni, J., Nguyen, C., Ampofo, B., Zhong, S. & Fei, Z. We classified these publications into the following four groups: resistance to biotic stresses, resistance to abiotic stresses, improvement of tomato fruit quality, and domestication of tomato.

Abstract
--------

Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats/CRISPR associated 9 (CRISPR/Cas9) system has recently become one popular technology due to its efficiency, precision, and simplicity compared with other genome editing tools such as [Zinc Finger Nucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/zinc-finger-nuclease "Learn more about Zinc Finger Nucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ZFNs) and [Transcription Activator Like Effector Nucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcription-activator-like-effector-nuclease "Learn more about Transcription Activator Like Effector Nucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (TALENs). [Horticultural crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/horticultural-crops "Learn more about Horticultural crops from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") provide energy and health-keeping nutrients to humankind. Genome-editing technology has become widely adopted in horticultural breeding with the increasing demand for high yield and better-quality horticultural crops. Here, we describe the CRISPR/Cas9 system construction, its optimization, including [sgRNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/guide-rna "Learn more about sgRNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") promoter, sgRNA design, [Cas9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cas9 "Learn more about Cas9 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[protein promoter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/promoter-protein "Learn more about protein promoter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), SpCas9 variants and orthologs, and vector delivery methods. We also summarized the application of this technology in horticultural plants for stress responses enhancement, fruit quality improvement, and cultivation traits modification. This detailed review was compiled to help establish comprehensive understanding of the CRISPR/Cas9 systems and provide a reference for further developing this technology to manipulate horticultural plant traits effectively.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Horticultural crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/horticultural-crops "Learn more about Horticultural crops from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") provide the nutritional support required for human life. Therefore, horticultural research and breeding should be constantly innovated to meet the increasing demand for high-yield and better-quality horticultural products. Traditional breeding of horticultural plants largely relies on natural variation of alleles and randomly mixed genes to obtain the desirable traits, and thus time-consuming, is less efficient, and labor-intensive, so new [breeding methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/breeding-method "Learn more about breeding methods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are urgently needed. [Genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about Genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") engineering breeding is the technology that has relieved this dilemma in recent decades, by introducing the desirable [recombinant DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/recombinant-dna "Learn more about recombinant DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into the [plant's genome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/plant-genome "Learn more about plant's genome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to provide new genotypes and traits for breeding purposes ([Parmar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0097); [Chen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0030)). However, the insertion of exogenous [recombinant DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/recombinant-dna "Learn more about recombinant DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into the plant genome sparked controversy over the safety of [transgenic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenics "Learn more about transgenic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") crops, and the transgenic breeding technology has been limited by the rigorous approval process for [transgenic crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenic-crop "Learn more about transgenic crops from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Wang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0134)). Therefore, looking for transfer DNA (T-DNA) insertion-free breeding technology draw the focus on gene-editing technologies. Scientists have developed several systems to introduce site-specific gene editing by using engineered [endonucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/endonuclease "Learn more about endonucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including [zinc finger nucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/zinc-finger-nuclease "Learn more about zinc finger nucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ZFNs), transcription activator-like effector [nucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nuclease "Learn more about nucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (TALENs), and Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats/CRISPR-associated 9 (CRISPR/Cas9). CRISPR/Cas9 technology has become one popular technology because of its efficiency, precision, and simplicity ([Xu et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0143)). The effective cassettes of the CRISPR/Cas9 vector are eliminated by self-crossing when the gene-editing is finished and thus accomplishes targeted [mutagenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mutagenesis "Learn more about mutagenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") without T-DNA insertion ([Zhang et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0154); [Zhou et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0158); Si et al., 2017; [Yang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0147), [2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0146)). The CRISPR/Cas9 technology is considered the most desirable choice to obtain genome-edited crops because of its lower cost, more flexibility, and high reliability. However, the CRISPR/Cas9 technology still has potential for improvement when used in horticultural crops. Therefore, we will discuss the evolution of gene-editing technologies, the construction of the CRISPR/Cas9 system, the optimization of the CRISPR/Cas9, and its application in horticultural crops, thus providing a reference for the technology development in horticultural crops traits improvement.

2. Types and mechanisms of gene-editing technologies
----------------------------------------------------

Gene-editing tools rely on engineered [endonucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/endonuclease "Learn more about endonucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to recognize the specific [DNA sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-sequence "Learn more about DNA sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") through their [DNA binding domain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dna-binding-domain "Learn more about DNA binding domain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (DBD) proteins or [guide RNAs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/guide-rna "Learn more about guide RNAs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and cleave the DNA sequence precisely and efficiently to make double-strand breaks (DSBs) in the specific target site. The DSBs are then repaired through two kinds of cellular [DNA repair](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-repair "Learn more about DNA repair from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") mechanisms, homology-directed repair (HDR) or error-prone non-homologous end-joining breaks (NHEJ), resulting in modifications of the target sites ([Wyman and Kanaar, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0136)). [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#tbl0001) provides the comparison between different gene-editing tools, and the schematic description of the mechanisms of different gene-editing technologies are shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#fig0001).

Customized ZFs arrays (in different colors) bind to the upstream and downstream of the target site and direct the _[FokI](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/foki "Learn more about FokI from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ restriction [nuclease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nuclease "Learn more about nuclease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to make a DSB on DNA ([Kim et al., 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0063); [Bibikova et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0014), [2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0015); [Urnov et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0127)). B. _Fok_ I cleaves the target sequence mediated by the designed TALE repeats (in different colors) ([Boch et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0018); [Morbitzer et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0090)). C. sgRNA containing 20-nt target [DNA sequences](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-sequence "Learn more about DNA sequences from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") directs the Cas9 to the PAM sequences (indicated by red lines) and cleaves the target DNA to achieve a DSB ([Jinek et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0059); [Cong et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0031)). Scissors indicate the cleavage sites of the engineered endonucleases.

[ZFN](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/zinc-finger-nuclease "Learn more about ZFN from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was the first engineered endonucleases with customized DBDs for specific gene editing ([Bibikova et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0014), [2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0015)). The DBD of [ZFN](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nuclease "Learn more about ZFN from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") consists of multiple Cys2-His2 zinc fingers (ZFs). Each ZF comprises almost 30 [amino acids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amino-acid "Learn more about amino acids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that recognize three nucleotides. Two pairs of designed ZF arrays recognizing the upstream and downstream sequences of target genes’ cleavage sites are fused with the non-specific [catalytic domain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-active-site "Learn more about catalytic domain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of endonuclease _Fok_ I to create a chimeric ZFN. The resulting ZFNs can produce DSBs in designated sites and cause mutations at target gene sequences ([Lloyd et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0078)) ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#fig0001), A).

Unlike ZFNs, [TALENs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcription-activator-like-effector-nuclease "Learn more about TALENs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") recognize specific DNA sequences through the DBD of transcription activator-like effectors (TALEs) secreted by the phytopathogenic bacteria [Xanthomonas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/xanthomonas "Learn more about Xanthomonas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([RÃ¶mer et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0101)). The DBD of TALEs contains 33–35 very conserved [amino acids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amino-acids "Learn more about amino acids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") repeats. The amino acids at the 12th and 13th positions of each repeat are highly variable, making the combination of these two amino acids determine the specificity to one single nucleotide, i.e., NI binds to A, NG binds to T, HD binds to C, and NN binds to G ([Boch et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0018); [Morbitzer et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0090)). Therefore, TALENs were customized to recognize different gene sequences through engineering the repeat units ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#fig0001), B).

ZFNs and TALENs are been gradually replaced by the new emerging genome-editing method CRISPR/Cas9. In the last decade, CRISPR/Cas9 technology has become the most widely used gene-editing technology due to its high efficiency, simplicity, and ease to use. The first CRISPR sequence was identified in _Escherichia coli_ in 1987 as the gene responsible for [isozyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/isozyme "Learn more about isozyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") conversion of [alkaline phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/alkaline-phosphatase "Learn more about alkaline phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Ishino et al., 1987](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0054)). Subsequently, a series of Cas proteins (endonucleases or cleaving proteins) were identified ([Jinek et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0059); [Zetsche et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0151); [Abudayyeh et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0001); [Cox et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0032); [Harrington et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0045)). The CRISPR/Cas system contains CRISPR [RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna "Learn more about RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (crRNA), trans-activating crRNA (tracrRNA), and Cas protein. crRNA contains a sequence homologous to the target gene and interacts with tracrRNA to form a single guide RNA (sgRNA) that directs the Cas protein to the target gene site to produce DSBs ([Jinek et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0059)). Based on the structure of Cas proteins, the CRISPR/Cas systems have been divided into two classes and six types: class 1 uses multi-protein complexes for [nucleic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nucleic-acid "Learn more about nucleic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cleavage, and class 2 uses the catalytic domains of a single protein for cleavage ([Koonin et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0066); [Makarova et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0085)). The type III systems are class 1 CRISPR/Cas systems, and the [ribonucleoprotein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ribonucleoprotein "Learn more about ribonucleoprotein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RNP) complexes of the type III system assemble different subunits that can cleave both RNA and single-strand DNA (ssDNA) ([Makarova et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0085)). The most widely used CRISPR/Cas9 system (type II in class 2) comes from [Streptococcus pyogenes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/streptococcus-pyogenes "Learn more about Streptococcus pyogenes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Barrangou et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0010)). Compared to other types of CRISPR systems, the CRISPR/Cas9 system has been engineered to work with two major components, a [Cas9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cas9 "Learn more about Cas9 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") endonuclease and a simplified sgRNA ([Sorek et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0112)). sgRNA is designed with a 20-nt DNA sequence upstream of the Cas9 recognition sequence: a short, trinucleotide (5′-NGG-3′ or 5′-NAG-3′) [protospacer adjacent motif](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protospacer-adjacent-motif "Learn more about protospacer adjacent motif from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PAM) in the target gene DNA. Cas9 protein moves to the target sequence under the guidance of sgRNA, recognizes PAM sequences, and then cleaves the 3 nt position upstream of the PAM sequence to produce a DSB, triggers the HDR pathway or NHEJ pathway to insert, delete, or replace DNA at the target site, achieving intracellular gene editing ([Sharma et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0111)) ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#fig0001), C). Instead of assembling [protein complexes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-complexes "Learn more about protein complexes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the CRISPR/Cas9 technology requires only a short [sgRNA sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna-sequence "Learn more about sgRNA sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to guide Cas9 to the target DNA for more efficient gene editing than ZFNs and TALENs. This technology has been widely adopted in medical and agricultural research ([Long et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0079); [Nelson et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0093); [Tabebordbar et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0116); [Grevet et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0042); [Lemmon et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0067); [Liu et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0077)) because of its low cost, design flexibility, and high efficiency.

3. The procedure of constructing CRISPR/Cas9 vector in horticultural plants
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Since CRISPR/Cas9 editing has been successfully applied in model species, such as [Arabidopsis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arabidopsis "Learn more about Arabidopsis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_thaliana_ and [Nicotiana benthamiana](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nicotiana-benthamiana "Learn more about Nicotiana benthamiana from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Li et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0068); [Nekrasov et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0092)), researchers have established this technology in [horticultural crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/horticultural-crops "Learn more about horticultural crops from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") recently. CRISPR/Cas9 system has been successfully applied in tomato ([Brooks et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0021)), orange ([Jia and Wang, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0057)), apple ([Malnoy et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0086); [Nishitani et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0094)), grape ([Malnoy et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0086); [Ren et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0103)), cucumber ([Chandrasekaran et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0025)), [watermelon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/watermelon "Learn more about watermelon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Tian et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0122)), strawberry ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0159)), banana ([Kaur et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0062)), [kiwifruit](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/kiwifruit "Learn more about kiwifruit from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Wang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0135)), carrot ([Klimek et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0065)), cacao ([Fister et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0041)), pear ([Charrier et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014122000462#bib0026)), yam ([Syombua et al., 2020](https://www.sc

CRISPR in Agriculture: 2024 in Review
=====================================

December 10, 2024[Perspectives](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/category/perspectives/)

 By  Tonio Chaparro 

Just 12 years after its development, the genome-editing tool [CRISPR](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/crispr/) is being used in a wide breadth of ways in plant and animal agriculture, from reducing waste to adapting plants and animals to climate change, from making plants that naturally resist weeds to ones that can be harvested more efficiently, from food to biofuels and paper. Each year, researchers are adapting CRISPR tools to be used in new species, for new purposes. In this article, we’ll go over the basics of genomic engineering in agriculture and then map out some of the most exciting new developments, like never-before-seen seedless berries, non-browning avocados, and THC-less hemp, as well as updates on research areas we covered in our [2022 review](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-agriculture-2022/) — plus what to watch for next!

**CRISPR MEETS AGRICULTURE**
----------------------------

Genetic modification has been used in varying forms for centuries to create crop plants with desired traits like making watermelons that are sweeter and with have fewer seeds, and domestic farm animals with traits like high milk production in cows. In the past decade, CRISPR has set itself apart as the go-to gene-editing technology largely due to its unparalleled speed, accuracy, and versatility.

![Image 10](blob:http://localhost/374ab754bc6acf184ee2941ef6c3e842)

For millennia, farmers — and more recently, scientists — have used a range of techniques to create crops with the traits we like.

The traditional CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing system can be likened to a pair of molecular scissors which scientists can program to cut the [DNA](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/dna/) double helix at specific locations in the [genome](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/genome/). Edits to plants and animals via CRISPR are typically introduced to “knockout,” or eliminate the function, of a particular [gene](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/gene/) to get a desired trait, but as you will see in this article, CRISPR can be used in a variety of ways. Learn more about the mechanics of CRISPR and uses in agriculture in our [CRISPRpedia](https://innovativegenomics.org/crisprpedia/) chapters on [CRISPR technology](https://innovativegenomics.org/crisprpedia/crispr-technology/) and [CRISPR in agriculture](https://innovativegenomics.org/crisprpedia/crispr-in-agriculture/).

The lengthy process of developing a genetically modified agricultural product, whether a plant, animal, or otherwise, starts in the lab with intensive research, optimization, and validation — a timeline that can take several years from start to finish. The process often includes examining performance in field-like conditions in a process known as field trials. These experiments can provide key evidence to regulators that a particular genetic modification will have the intended effect and pose minimal risks to the natural environment or human health. The product is then generally submitted to regulatory agencies (e.g., the USDA) for review. Upon approval and deregulation, the developer may then begin the marketing, scaling, and distributing to growers or consumers. Similar processes are in place all over the world with slight variations about what criteria must be met prior to an approval. Note that some countries require producers to prove that their product has the intended edit and does not contain any genes that come from a different organism.

We’ll break down exciting new developments using CRISPR in agriculture and for each example we’ll note how far along in development the product has come, from initial research stages to completed field trials.

**GRAPHIC SUMMARY**
-------------------

![Image 12](blob:http://localhost/1857002f4e72f302aeab32ceac32e6d3)

The progress of CRISPR-edited agricultural products covered in this article from lab research to being commercially available. As shown in the image, at the time of writing, some of the products are poised between stages: CRISPR-edited teff is ready for field-testing, while several products (non-browning bananas, early-flowering cowpeas, slick-coat cattle, and non-psychoactive cannabis) have undergone field testing but have not yet been commercialized.

**EDITING FOR THE EATER**
-------------------------

CRISPR is being used to edit crop plants to deliver on wide-ranging food interests for consumers. Whether it’s optimizing taste or texture, enhancing the nutritional profile, or extending the shelf life of some of our favorite foods, the future of food is closer than we might imagine. This section spotlights projects harnessing CRISPR to engineer better potatoes for chipping, seedless blackberries, and non-browning bananas and avocados! We’ll provide brief updates on a few products introduced in our [2022 article](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-agriculture-2022/).

### **Putting the CRISP in CRISPR**

![Image 14: poplar icon](blob:http://localhost/c5584474da75a28c59da342a20a74847)
[Potatoes](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36679094/) put in cold-storage have the tendency to accumulate chemical precursors which, upon high-temperature treatment — like frying — can be converted to the potentially carcinogenic compound acrylamide. To give you a friendlier French fry, researchers at Murdoch University in Western Australia introduced a CRISPR-Cas9 system to one of the most popular potato “chipping” cultivars, Atlantic, and used it to then disrupt the genes responsible for the synthesis of these precursors. Their edited potatoes showed a dramatic reduction in the chemical precursors after cold-storage. The team even went a step further and found that chips (“_crisps”_ for our friends down under) made from these edited potato varieties had up to 80% less acrylamide. One bag of sea salt and vinegar, please!

### **Needless to say, seedless blackberries are on their way**

![Image 16: poplar icon](blob:http://localhost/c5584474da75a28c59da342a20a74847)
Pairwise, the company that developed the first CRISPR-edited food available in North America (more to come on those leafy greens!), made a splash in Summer 2024 when they announced the advancements they’ve made in engineering the world’s first [seedless blackberries](https://www.pairwise.com/news/pairwise-develops-first-seedless-blackberry). To achieve this, scientists at Pairwise used proprietary CRISPR technology to target genes responsible for the tough seed pits found in blackberries. The resulting blackberries, though not _truly_ seedless, contain softer, chewy seeds similar to what we find in our favorite “seedless” grapes and watermelon. To further improve this variety, other edits were made to eliminate thorns and allow the plant to grow more compactly. The “stacking” of these traits is common for plant breeders, in an effort to make varieties that are better for both consumers and growers. These seedless, thornless, compact blackberries have entered field trials and are working towards upscaled commercialization. The toothpick industry should be scared.

We can’t deny it — we love avocado toast.

### **Non-browning avocados**

![Image 20: Pig icon](blob:http://localhost/b498165fb6606fbfddf5281c104420b6)
“Refrigerate! Keep the pit! Lime juice!” The age-old question of how to keep your [avocados](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/ag-biotech-innovator-greenvenus-achieves-breakthrough-in-non-browning-avocado-through-gene-editing-301842939.html) green and fresh rages on. Scientists at GreenVenus are looking to put this debate to rest by engineering a non-browning avocado using CRISPR technology. Following in the footsteps of what was previously accomplished in apple, banana, and mushrooms, this non-browning avocado was achieved by disrupting an [enzyme](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/enzyme/), polyphenol oxidase, crucial to the browning process. Although yet to be scaled, GreenVenus reported success in multiple commercial varieties and hopes this breakthrough will help reduce food waste by improving the avocado’s shelf life. Soon we’ll have no choice but to eat that week-old avocado in our kitchens… Guac, anyone?

### **Updates: The first CRISPR-edited product hits US markets & more**

![Image 22: Mustard Greens](blob:http://localhost/4ad57241c93f06620c98860531ccdd03)
**_Bitterless mustard greens, the first CRISPR-edited product in American grocery stores_**In our [2022 review](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-agriculture-2022/), we introduced you to what would become the first CRISPR-edited product on the North American market: [bitterless mustard greens](https://www.pairwise.com/news/the-first-crispr-edited-salad-is-here)! The distribution of these leafy greens was initially limited to a handful of food service locations nationwide. The company behind the development, Pairwise, recently struck a deal with Bayer to expand commercialization of the ground-breaking green and bring it to a grocery store near you.

![Image 24: Pig icon](blob:http://localhost/b498165fb6606fbfddf5281c104420b6)
**_Non-browning banana given a green light in Philippines_**While we patiently await ongoing commercialization efforts to bring [non-browning bananas](https://www.freshfruitportal.com/news/2024/07/03/new-non-browning-bananas-in-the-philippines/) to our local produce section, Tropic Biosciences — the company behind the CRISPR-edited fruit — has been busy spreading the word. During the summer of 2024, the Philippines Department of Agriculture and Bureau of Plant Industry deemed the edited bananas non-GMO and gave the green light for import and propagation.

![Image 26: Pig icon](blob:http://localhost/b498165fb6606fbfddf5281c104420b6)
**_Vitamin D tomato_**Initial work engineered edits to overproduce precursors for vitamin D which would accumulate after exposure to UV light in lab [tomatoes](https://www.jic.ac.uk/press-release/gene-edited-tomatoes-could-be-a-new-source-of-vitamin-d/#:~:text=Tomatoes%20naturally%20contain%20one%20of,leaves%20at%20very%20low%20levels.). Expect similar modifications to be applied to commercial tomato varieties next, as well as other vegetable relatives from the nightshade family like eggplants, potato, and peppers.

**EDITING FOR FARMERS**
-----------------------

Current levels of crop production are unlikely to meet the projected doubling of global food demand by 2050. Among the multitude of challenges farmers face, pest management in crops and the spread of infectious diseases among livestock are two challenges that can result in suboptimal yield for growers. This section presents exciting new projects using CRISPR to boost resilience to pests and disease, as well as optimizing crop architecture to make harvesting easier. We’ll also bring you brief updates on products mentioned in our past [article](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-agriculture-2022/).

### **Witch, please…**

![Image 28: poplar icon](blob:http://localhost/c5584474da75a28c59da342a20a74847)
[Sorghum](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-00176-8) is a fundamental cereal crop grown globally, providing a [major source](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crop-of-the-future-sorghum/) of grain for food and feedstock. In Africa, however, sorghum yield is threatened by the omnipresent, parasitic plant _Striga hermonthica,_ also know as “witchweed,” seeds of which can be found in over half of available farmland. Chemical compounds from the roots of sorghum trigger the growth of witchweed seeds which then promptly invade the sorghum root system to sneakily swindle water and nutrients at the expense of the sorghum host. Naturally resistant wild sorghum varieties do exist and are able to withstand infestation due to gene variants which change the compounds that normally help witchweed seeds grow. Researchers at Kenyatta University in Nairobi, Kenya are aiming to recapitulate these helpful mutations in domesticated sorghum varieties using CRISPR with hopes it will provide some resistance to infestation without relying on the presence of transgenes or foreign DNA. Edited sorghum seeds underwent field trials this summer, making them one of the first to hit African soil. Double, double, withweed’s in trouble!

More fish, less antibiotics

### **Catfish with a side of alligator, *chef’s kiss***

![Image 32: poplar icon](blob:http://localhost/c5584474da75a28c59da342a20a74847)
In aquaculture, loss due to infectious diseases can put a huge financial burden on fish farmers. Antibiotics are routinely applied to treat outbreaks but over-reliance increases the risk of developing resistant bacterial strains and can have adverse effects on human health. A promising alternative approach is to use antimicrobial peptide genes or AMGs, which integrate into the host’s genome and provide improved resistance to a suite of pathogens. Historically, transgenic introduction of AMGs has relied on inserting the AMGs at a random spot in the genome which can lead to problems if it is lands in an existing gene or locus of importance. In a recent proof-of-concept approach, a research group out of Auburn University harnessed a CRISPR-Cas9 system to insert an alligator-derived AMG known as cathelicidin into the genome of [blue catfish](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0044848623006348?via%3Dihub) in a specific location in the genome. To achieve this specificity and prevent the potential for unwanted spread of the [transgene](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/transgene/), the cathelicidin gene was inserted in a spot that would stop female catfish from reaching reproductive maturity unless treated with external hormones. In other words, the catfish with AMG will only be able to reproduce and pass on their genetic material in a highly controlled manner (i.e., aquaculture breeding). The research team showed improved survival and bacterial resistance among transgenic blue catfish and had no impact on overall growth or morphology. Transgenic approaches such as this inherently come with more regulatory hurdles, but this new line of disease-resistance blue catfish can help aquaculturists mitigate the use of traditional antibiotics and sets a precedent for engineering transgenic livestock via modern gene-editing techniques. Who’s coming to the fish fry??

### **Early-flowering cowpeas make mechanized harvest possible**

![Image 36: poplar icon](blob:http://localhost/c5584474da75a28c59da342a20a74847)
[Cowpeas](https://www.prnewswire.com/il/news-releases/first-successful-gene-editing-of-cowpea---israeli-genetics--seeds-company-betterseeds-redesigns-architecture-of-cowpea-plant-to-enable-its-mechanized-harvesting-301730441.html), or black-eyed peas, are a tropical crop which provide a vital source of [protein](https://innovativegenomics.org/glossary/protein/) and carbohydrates for both humans and livestock. While many crops stop growing once they reach sexual maturity, a defining feature of some field-grown cowpea varieties is their ability to grow continuously. This causes them to flower and develop seeds in an asynchronous manner which makes harvesting in bulk difficult to manage. In early 2023, scientists at BetterSeeds announced their intentions to begin field trials of the first gene-edited cowpea that allows for mechanized harvest. To achieve this, proprietary technology was used to introduce CRISPR editing tools that targeted genes responsible for plant architecture and flowering time. The resulting edited cowpea plants grew stronger vertically and flowered in sync, making mechanized harvest possible. These bushy cowpeas were deregulated by the USDA late last year. MOOoove over soybean, there’s a new bean in town.

### **Update: Anti-lodging teff passes USDA regulatory review**

![Image 38: Pig icon](blob:http://localhost/b498165fb6606fbfddf5281c104420b6)
In our [2022 review](https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-agriculture-2022/), we described the gene-editing approaches being taken in teff, a vital grain crop in Ethiopia, to reduce losses due to “lodging,” the process in which stems buckle under the weight of heavy grains near the top of the plant. The USDA has since deemed that the edits introduced to develop this anti-lodging teff are unlikely to pose any increased risks and have deregulated their use. Next up are field trials!

**EDITING FOR A DYNAMIC PLANET**
--------------------------------

In 2023, global temperatures reached their highest in recorded history and 2024 is on pace to take over as the single hottest year on record. This unprecedented increase in temperature is closely linked to human-caused emission of greenhouse gasses. Increased temperatures usher in unpredictably severe weather patterns, droughts, and saltier soils — all of which put global agricultural productivity at risk. We previously highlighted gene-editing efforts to mitigate these effects which included boosting heat tolerance in cattle and improving disease resistance in wheat and pigs. This section will feature ongoing projects to develop crops for alternative fuel sources and better suited to a changing climate.

enriched_points.expanded_content: CRISPR technology has successfully edited genomes of various horticultural crops, such as tomatoes and strawberries, improving traits like disease resistance and fruit quality.
Significant enhancements in crop traits have been reported, including increased lycopene levels in tomatoes and improved shelf life and nutritional value in various fruits.
Despite its potential, public skepticism about CRISPR technology persists, primarily due to safety concerns and the historical context of genetically modified organisms (GMOs).
